questions.specification:
A city council has commissioned a new self-service ticketing kiosk for a busy transport hub. The kiosk must support commuters buying single and return tickets, tourists purchasing day passes, and passengers who need step-free route information.

The first prototype includes:
- a $12$-inch touchscreen with small text and icons
- an on-screen keyboard for entering destinations
- contactless payment only
- a receipt printer positioned low on the right-hand side
- a help button that opens a long FAQ page

During a short pilot, staff observed queues building at peak times and frequent errors when users selected the wrong ticket type. Some users tried to tap their cards multiple times, and several left without completing the purchase.

A designer produced a sketch to summarise the current layout, see Figure 1.

![Sketch of a standing ticket kiosk: large touchscreen at eye level showing dense menu lists; small help icon in the top corner; contactless reader to the left of the screen; receipt slot low on the right; no physical buttons; a nearby sign says "No cash".](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/51eac401-e698-474d-936e-7b5a421a8568.png)

Figure 1: Early kiosk layout used in the pilot

The council wants the next iteration to be demonstrably usable, inclusive, and safe to deploy across multiple stations with different lighting and noise levels.

parts.content: Outline one reason why usability is important to the success of this kiosk in the transport hub environment.
parts.markscheme:
- Links usability to increased completion of purchases / reduced abandonment in a high-pressure setting (e.g., queues, time-critical travel). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links usability to reduced operational cost or risk (e.g., fewer staff interventions, fewer payment errors/chargebacks, fewer complaints). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline one reason why efficiency is an important usability objective for this kiosk.
parts.markscheme:
- Efficiency reduces time per transaction, decreasing queue length at peak times. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Efficiency lowers cognitive/physical effort (fewer steps/taps), improving throughput and reducing frustration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline one reason why error tolerance is important for this kiosk.
parts.markscheme:
- Prevents incorrect ticket purchases by reducing slip/mistake likelihood (e.g., wrong ticket type) or enabling easy correction (undo/back/confirm). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces financial/time impact and support burden (refunds, disputes, staff assistance) and improves user confidence. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Suggest how the kiosk interface could be redesigned using design guidance to make it more usable for a wide range of passengers.
parts.markscheme:
- Proposes clearer information hierarchy (e.g., large primary actions such as “Buy ticket”, “Day pass”, “Plan route”) to support recognition over recall. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses appropriate typography/contrast/spacing (e.g., larger font, high contrast, consistent icon+label) to improve readability and accessibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces steps and input burden (e.g., fewer screens, destination suggestions, recent/popular destinations, postcode scan) to improve efficiency. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adds error-prevention/recovery patterns (e.g., confirmation before payment, prominent back/undo, clear summary screen) to improve safety and error tolerance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides effective help and feedback (e.g., contextual help, progress indicators, immediate feedback on card tap, timeouts with warning) to improve satisfaction and learnability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how the design team could improve the kiosk’s usability using a user-centred approach, making reference to at least three usability principles and appropriate design guidance.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains recruiting representative users and contexts (e.g., commuters, tourists, wheelchair users, low vision; different lighting/noise; peak/off-peak) to ensure findings reflect real use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains task-based usability testing with realistic scenarios (e.g., buy a return to X, purchase a day pass, change ticket type, request step-free route) rather than abstract questioning. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- References learnability and how to measure/improve it (e.g., first-time success rate, time-to-complete on first attempt; clearer labels; guided flow). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- References efficiency and how to measure/improve it (e.g., taps/screens per purchase, transaction time; streamline navigation; defaults for common journeys). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- References effectiveness and how to measure/improve it (e.g., completion rate, correct ticket selection; clearer categorisation; confirmation screens). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- References error tolerance and how to measure/improve it (e.g., error counts, recoveries; undo/back; validation; prevent double-tap payment issues). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses design guidance to justify interface choices (e.g., consistency, visibility of system status, clear feedback on payment, accessibility guidelines for contrast/target size). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes iterative cycle: test, analyse issues, redesign, re-test; shows how evidence informs changes rather than one-off evaluation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links improved usability to importance in deployment (e.g., reduced queues/support needs, improved public trust, safer inclusive access). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

options.markscheme: Feature W (a flashing red light) describes **feedback**, not mapping.
Feature X (layout of dials matches geographical arrangement) is **natural mapping**, i.e. the relationship between controls and their effects matches the system being controlled.
Feature Y (a plastic cover) is a **constraint** (physical barrier), not mapping.
Feature Z (a small notch) is a **constraint** (forces a particular alignment/fit), not mapping.

questions.specification:
The electric seat adjustment controls in a vehicle are designed in the shape of a miniature seat. Moving the 'backrest' portion of the control switch causes the actual car seat backrest to move in the same direction.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-dfe6b99d-08d2-4d94-8862-1b75d512cdbe-1771929956947/db70330c-9c01-4def-8e31-f4b32aae4142.jpeg)

Which usability principle is primarily demonstrated by this interface design?

**AquaPure**

A new company, AquaPure, aims to introduce a line of advanced water filtration systems for home use. These systems are designed to be eco-friendly, easy to install, and cost-effective. AquaPure wants to ensure that their product meets consumer needs and stands out in a competitive market. 

They are focusing on sustainable materials, innovative filtration technology, and user-friendly design. AquaPure needs to conduct thorough market research to ensure the product meets consumer needs and preferences.

parts.content: Identify **two** market research strategies that AquaPure could use to gather information about consumer preferences for water filtration systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Use surveys/questionnaires to collect data on desired features, material preferences and acceptable price range <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use focus groups to gather qualitative feedback through discussion of preferences and opinions about water filtration systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how AquaPure could use perceptual mapping to position their water filtration system in the market.
parts.markscheme:
- Define perceptual mapping as a visual representation of consumer perceptions of products/brands in a market <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that AquaPure plots competitor products against key attributes (e.g., price, sustainability, filtration performance) to see relative positioning and gaps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link the identified gap/desired position to how AquaPure can emphasise differentiating features in its marketing/design to appeal to the target market <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe **two** advantages of conducting a competitor analysis for AquaPure before launching their water filtration system.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify market opportunities by comparing competitors’ strengths/weaknesses to find ways to differentiate (features, quality, sustainability, price) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduce risk/improve decision-making by informing pricing, marketing and product features based on competitor strategies and positioning <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: AquaPure decides to conduct a usability testing session for their new water filtration system design. Outline the key steps they should follow in this process.
parts.markscheme:
- Define the objectives/criteria for the test (e.g., ease of installation, ease of use, satisfaction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recruit suitable participants from the target market and prepare realistic tasks/scenarios <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Run the session: brief participants, observe them completing tasks, and record issues/feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyse findings, prioritise problems, and implement improvements (iterate if needed) <marks>1 mark</marks>
